media:content.media:description: Comparing the sizes of the Samsung Galaxy Z Flip 8 (pink) with the white Galaxy Z Fold 8 Ultra and grey Galaxy Z Fold 8
media:content.media:text: Comparing the sizes of the Samsung Galaxy Z Flip 8 (pink) with the white Galaxy Z Fold 8 Ultra and grey Galaxy Z Fold 8
media:content.media:title: Comparing the sizes of the Samsung Galaxy Z Flip 8 (pink) with the white Galaxy Z Fold 8 Ultra and grey Galaxy Z Fold 8

item.content:encoded:
Samsung Galaxy Unpacked is here! Managing Editor Derrek Lee is in the U.K. for the event and is reporting on all things foldables, watches, and everything else that launched today.

This marks the first time in 14 years that Samsung is back in the U.K. for a big announcement (the last time was the Galaxy S3!), and Samsung is launching tons of new products, including a new Fold that's ready to take the world by storm. Scroll down to see everything announced today!

When and what time is Samsung Unpacked?

The event is today, July 22nd, and it starts at 9am EST (New York time)...in the U.K. the time will be 2pm BST.

What was announced at Samsung Galaxy Unpacked?

  • A duo of new Galaxy Z Fold 8 phones has been announced, including a new shorter, wider model that's expected to be the best-selling Galaxy Z Fold to date. Alongside it, Samsung debuted the Galaxy Z Fold 8 Ultra, which looks nearly identical to the Z Fold 7, with a taller aspect ratio and three cameras on the back. Likely, this naming change is to line up with Apple's expected iPhone Ultra, the company's first folding phone, which is expected to be announced in September.
  • The Galaxy Z Flip 8 brings back Samsung's iconic flip phones, with a slimmer design and upgraded specs.
  • Samsung also brought a refresh to its watch line, including the Galaxy Watch 9 and the Galaxy Watch Ultra 2. The Watch 9 looks almost identical to the Ultra 2 from many angles, including a circle-within-a-squircle design and big new features.
  • Lastly, Samsung debuted its first smart glasses with Warby Parker and Gentle Monster designs. These are powered by Gemini and include cameras on the front and speakers on each arm.

What's the best way to watch Samsung Galaxy Unpacked?

There are three ways to watch Samsung's Galaxy Unpacked event live. You can tune in via Samsung's official websites, including Samsung.com and Samsung Newsroom.

Perhaps the easiest way to watch the event is on YouTube. Head to Samsung's official YouTube channel or watch from the link below.

We’re reaching the final countdown! Samsung’s Unpacked event is just hours away, and I’m currently on the ground in London to experience it all in real-time. Be sure to tune in at 9 a.m.ET to watch the event and keep this tab open for our play-by-play and real-time thoughts on Samsung’s new devices and announcements!

I'll send over more pictures from the event, but for now, here's a shot of the event location from last year!!

What am I most excited to see at Unpacked? I’m really curious about the Galaxy Z Fold 8 and its new wide aspect ratio.

I really want to see how apps look on the external display and how it handles multitasking on the internal folding screen, because frankly, I’m not convinced that this is an optimal design for a foldable phone. It feels like Samsung is trying to replicate the Galaxy Z TriFold's internal aspect ratio, but it comes off as an awkward attempt. Idk, I guess we’ll see when it's announced and I get my hands on it.

I'm so excited to be here, and London is looking great! Like I said, we are just a few hours away from the event and there's so much going on. I landed here on Sunday, but we've been in briefings all week.

There are a LOOOOT of exciting things happening and I can't wait to share it all at 9am ET when the event starts!!

The Galaxy Z Fold 8 should take center stage at the event, and it's exciting for a lot of reasons. A shorter and wider foldable makes it easier to consume content, and it looks like Samsung is leaning heavily into this, if leaked marketing is anything to go by. Of course, a smaller foldable is also easier to hold and use, and if you've used a Surface Duo or the Find N2 in the past, you should be excited about the Z Fold 8.

Samsung is seeing increased demand for its foldables, and not just in the U.S. These devices have been around for well over seven years now, and the tech has matured to a point where its mainstream. The Galaxy Z Fold 7 had a lot to do with this, as the thinner and lighter design made it that much more enticing an upgrade if you're using a Galaxy S device.

I love flip phones, so I would’ve loved to see Samsung bring back another better Galaxy Z Flip FE, but it doesn’t look like that’s the case.

To be honest, it makes sense, as book-style foldables have been on the rise and have overtaken flip phones in sales volume. That seems to be why Samsung is set to offer two Fold variants this time around; to hopefully appeal to more people.

Will it work?

One thing Samsung does great is accessories. The company is great at launching a bevy of cases, screen protectors, and bands for its devices, and I expect the same treatment for the foldables and watches. It’s likely there will be a selection of magnetic cases available since we don’t expect any of the devices to feature built-in Qi2 magnets.

We’ll be sure to highlight some of the best ones we find when the devices launch so you can pair your new purchase with some much-needed protection.

I also really hope we get to see more of Samsung’s Android XR glasses. We’ve seen Google's prototypes, and I got to play with Xreal’s display glasses, but I want to get my hands on Samsung’s production glasses. They’re expected to launch this fall, so with any luck, we’ll hear more about them at Unpacked, but they’re probably my most anticipated launch of this year.

And don’t get me wrong, I like Meta’s glasses, but there’s a bit of a disconnect. I also prefer Gemini to Meta AI, so it would be cool to have some stylish glasses that sync with my phone and watch.

And while the Meta glasses have been an amazing addition to my tech bag, and I love the hands-free recording to capture amazing memories while being present with my family and friends...a few jerks have ruined the public image of these glasses for everyone.

So how will Google and Samsung get around the stigma of “pervert glasses” that the AI glasses category now finds itself in? That’s a great question, and Google has only answered part of it so far.

In a recent interview with our sister site Tom’s Guide, Google's XR product management lead, Juston Payne, explained that Google and Samsung are doing everything in their power to create a system of trust around its upcoming AI glasses. That includes LED indicators on the front that illuminate when recording starts, as well as “employing Android permission frameworks to help protect users.”

Meta already employs LED indicators on its AI glasses and recently rolled out a mandatory glasses update that permanently disables the camera if the LED is tampered with. Meta doesn’t have the same level of Android OS-level controls that Google does, though, so we’re thinking the company could allow users to enable automated alerts when a pair of glasses is recording nearby, similar to how Android users can enable warnings when Bluetooth trackers are nearby.

So far, Google and Samsung’s upcoming glasses look to be mostly a carbon copy of what Meta has built over the years, but that’s not necessarily a bad thing. Like Meta, Google and Samsung are working with fashion brands (Gentle Monster and Warby Parker this time instead of Ray-Ban and Oakley) to make a fashion-forward pair of glasses that also happen to have cool features inside.

And these glasses will be powered by Gemini, not Meta AI, which may make all the difference in the world to a lot of people. Meta AI is used a lot around the world, but lots of people I know prefer Gemini because it integrates so well with Google’s services. I would say that Google is also slightly more trusted than Meta, which is another positive point for what Samsung is expected to show off today.

Whenever a company shows off AI glasses, it always talks about translation features and how great they are. That’s fine and all, but I don’t find myself needing translation features every day, or even every month. In fact, the only time I’ve used the translation features on any glasses I own is when I was reviewing features and seeing how well they work.

But Google’s announced navigation features might be a different story for me entirely! In the Tom’s Guide interview, Google’s XR product management lead, Juston Payne, said, “The audio experience for [navigation] is fantastic,” Payne said. “It uses the camera to localize you, so it knows that you're pointing in the right direction. It can give you contextual navigation to help you get to where you're going without being vague, you know, like ‘turn right there.’”
You can do some of this with Google Maps on your phone, but I don’t care to hold my phone up constantly while walking around. Having this on my glasses would provide yet another hands-free opportunity, letting me get lost in the moment without actually getting lost.

While we expect to see Samsung’s AI glasses, Google seems more comfortable with calling them “intelligent eyewear” rather than “AI glasses.” This is an interesting choice of language because, at least to me, it seems to imply that people are less interested in yet another AI gadget, but are likely still receptive to a cool gadget that looks great in the process.

Whether people will actually use all the nifty Gemini-powered features is another story entirely. I almost never use the AI functions on my Meta or Rokid glasses despite them being a simple voice command away. For the most part, these kinds of glasses are most useful to me as a camera and as a way to get notifications without visual distractions. I’d love to see if Google’s implementation convinces me otherwise.

What about colors for the Galaxy Z Flip 8? Well, rumors claim the flip foldable could debut in Cream, Graphite, Mint, and Pink.

This is an interesting assortment, for sure. If you’re asking me (and, even if you're not, I’m asking me), I really only vibe with Graphite. I can get down with pink, too, if I’m honest. It’s different. It pops.

I’m going to be real: these rumored colors for Samsung’s foldables are passable at best for me. I mean, am I a crazy person if I like vibrant primary colors? Where’s my red Fold 8 or Flip 8? I would absolutely love a red foldable from Samsung. Crimson would be a sick color, too. I’m not saying these colors (if they’re real) are bad. All I’m saying is Samsung could do better (or do different).

There’s a lot that we’re expecting from Samsung’s Unpacked this summer. Personally, I’m most interested in the brand’s smartwatches. The Galaxy Watch 9, seeing how the company is positioning it as an “always-on” device is intriguing. Samsung made efforts to boost its heart health features, then it rolled out for the user’s hearing. Its full presentation will certainly be one that I’ll focus on, for sure.

Going back to the AI glasses...I want to see how well it will play with Samsung’s Galaxy phones should the lenses appear during the keynote. Samsung’s AI has been all over its phones. It wants to be helpful, it wants to help make mundane tasks feel effortless.

So, where do its glasses fit in? How will these glasses do better than the phone when it’s in your pocket? Will it expand what we already have in our hands and put it on our faces?

We are about 15 minutes away from the Big SHOW! What are you most excited about?? Answer the poll above!

And the show is beginning! Samsung is going all-in on this new aspect ratio, with tons of teasers ahead of the start. Get ready!

Samsung is doubling down on what it calls "the importance of AI infrastructure," linking all its products together with Galaxy AI as it says it's bringing the service to 800 million devices by the end of this year. Of course, that begins with new devices like Galaxy Glasses (that's the official name, right?), pictured above.

Time for foldables! Samsung's TM Roh is on stage talking about Samsung's leadership in foldables, saying the company is the global leader in foldables, and this year's new devices will only further that lead.

If you haven't had a chance to read our hands-on with the Galaxy Z Fold 8 series, go ahead and do that now! Android Central Managing Editor, Derrek Lee, got to check out the new phones ahead of Unpacked and has tons of photos and info for you to check out.

A suave gentleman with a lovely British accent just took the stage to announce the Galaxy Z Fold 8 Ultra, which he says "represents the very best of Samsung Galaxy."

The Fold 8 Ultra looks pretty darn similar to the Z Fold 7, and Samsung says this year's remains the thinnest Galaxy phone ever despite the visual similarities.

The Z Fold 8 Ultra is made thinner thanks to Flex Titanium, a new screen structure that's thinner and stronger than the previous plastic-based flexible glass structure. Samsung also swapped out the hinge with something thinner, lighter, and even stronger, delivering further improvements alongside the expected evolution of the Z Fold series.

I could have done without the AI-generated hamster brainrot, but Samsung's battery announcement is a BIG one. At 5,000mAh, the Z Fold 8 Ultra packs the highest capacity battery ever in a Z Fold phone, with 5% silicon-carbon content. This enables faster charging, and while neither capacity or charging speed are quite matching the company's biggest competition, we VERY MUCH welcome any improvement in this area on Samsung's foldables.

The Z Fold 8 Ultra's upgraded cameras can record 8k video, take "stunning Nightography shots," and features a substantially upgraded ultrawide sensor at 50MP.

Samsung is talking about improved performance on the Z Fold 8 Ultra, including enhanced Galaxy AI performance and even better gaming performance than in the past. That's not just a normal silicon upgrade with a faster processor, it means Galaxy AI is able to intelligently scale things to keep your phone cool and fast over time.

Now Nudge is coming to the Z Fold series! This means proactive buttons that appear alongside your text and favorite apps, giving you instant ways to add new entries to your calendar and open apps side-by-side with context-sensitive suggestions.

And that's the Galaxy Z Fold 8 Ultra! This one starts at a rather painful $2,099 this year, so you'll definitely want to grab those preorder deals once they're available following the show.

And here's the Z Fold 8! It's a new shorter, wider Galaxy Z Fold, and I couldn't be more excited about this new size!

The new aspect ratio on the outer display still works very well with vertical video (surprisingly) thanks to some small borders on the left and right side.

Unfold the Z Fold 8 and you get a lovely 4:3 aspect ratio, which Samsung says is perfect for watching content and reading the comments at the same time.

Gaming is better thanks to the aspect ratio, which gives you a much more hand-friendly size to game on.

Also, WHEN DID PUBG GET A NARUTO EVENT???

Samsung says the new shape is also perfect for reading, as you just rotate the inner display to get a lovely page-shaped design. I'm not sure I want to be reading anything long-term on Samsung's low-PWM displays, but I certainly welcome the new shape change!

Samsung pits this one as the world's lightest Fold (that's book-style folds, if you're wondering), complete with a 4,800mAh battery and the same Flex Titanium tech as the Z Fold 8 Ultra.

A new camera mode will automatically follow someone and frame them perfectly in a vertical format, so you can do less editing and more posting.

And there's the Z Fold 8, a new shorter, wider Fold that's lighter and easier to use than ever. This one starts at $1,899 and will be available from August 7.

Time for the Galaxy Z Flip 8! This one's lighter and slimmer than ever at just 6.1mm unfolded and around 180g.

The real highlight of the new release is the upgraded Flex Window, which is brought about with One UI 9 (that means older Flips get it too). This includes new gestures, full notifications and quick toggles, and more apps that open properly on the cover screen. Just don't expect something that's quite Motorola level without downloading Multistar, though.

The Z Flip 8 ships with Samsung's awesome Horizon Lock feature, so that camcorder recording is smoother than ever.

And here's all the specs for the Z Flip 8! This one starts at $1,199 and launches alongside the Folds on Aug 7. Preorders start TODAY!

Ned from the latest Spider-Man movies took the stage to show off his latest invention, the Spidey Tracker, running side-by-side with camera footage on a Galaxy Z Fold 8 Ultra.

The AI-generated brainrot is back! Ignoring the annoying part, Samsung Care+ is being shown off as a big feature for the new Fold and Flip phones, which will be a big deal if Apple ends up launching a folding phone as we expect them to this year.

Samsung is trying to get you to sign up for its new credit card with discounts on preorders for the new phones. I'll pass, but some people in the audience sounded rather enthused.

Galaxy Watch 9 leans further into the circle-within-a-squircle design and brings about a ton of new "proactive care" features powered by Galaxy AI.

The new proactive features constantly monitor heart rate, heart rate variability, respiratory rate, skin temperature, and blood oxygen, combining all these things into a new heart health score.

Heart health metrics give you tons of information and, more importantly, ways to help reduce problems when they arise.

Samsung is partnering with iFit to bring custom workouts to your watch, matching these workouts up with its new heart health metrics and existing metrics technologies to give you ways to improve your health at home rather than generic workouts.

Samsung announced that the Galaxy Watch 9 and Galaxy Ring will be the first FDA-approved pair to monitor sleep apnea! That's a huge deal and, quite frankly, the first time I've seen a good reason to get a Galaxy Ring.

Time for the Galaxy Watch Ultra 2! It might look similar, but it's got a TON of upgrades over the original Watch Ultra, including a new Snapdragon Elite processor with 32% better performance and a 35% larger battery.

The Watch Ultra 2 is the strongest device Samsung has ever made, with IP69K and 10 ATM ratings, as well as a new dive rating.

Samsung is partnering with Mares to deliver exclusive diving computer features the company says will only be avaialble on the Watch Ultra 2. This exclusivity lasts for the next 2 years and makes the Watch Ultra 2 the smartwatch to get for divers.

The Watch Ultra 2 can detect when you're sweating and make intelligent suggestions to drink more water when you're getting dehydrated. The watch uses a bevy of metrics to do this, and should be a huge deal for athletes and runners.

Here are all the specs for the Galaxy Watch 9! This one starts at $379 for the Bluetooth-only versions and will be available on August 7.

Ready for Galaxy Watch Ultra 2 specs? You're in luck, because they're quite impressive, and the watch is heading to store shelves on August 7 from $699.

Rick Osterloh is taking the stage to talk about Gemini Intelligence, including the new next-gen task automation features. We got to check these out in our hands-on with the Z Fold 8 series.

Task Automation is coming to 40 more apps, which means you can ask Gemini to do something for you instead of spending time doing it yourself. Gemini will open the app in the background and get things done, then alert you when it's finished.

Gemini Intelligence is also coming to Samsung and Google's upcoming smart glasses, which the company calls "Intelligent Eyewear."

Qualcomm has taken the stage, talking about the Snapdragon 8 Elite Gen 5 for Galaxy processor inside Samsung's Z Fold 8 and Fold 8 Ultra. We've already seen this processor in the S26 series, and now it's headed to Samsung's foldables for the first time.

The Snapdragon Wear Elite in the Galaxy Watch Ultra 2 isn't just faster, it also helps the watch deliver more advanced health metrics thanks to faster, better sensors and better Bluetooth connectivity and interoperability.

If you haven't taken a look at our Galaxy Watch 9 and Watch Ultra 2 hands-on yet, now is a great time to do that!

Samsung's Intelligent Eyewear has taken the stage!

We've already known about the Warby Parker and Gentle Monster partnership for about a year now, but it's becoming real now that the glasses are about to launch.

Samsung talked a lot about how these glasses were engineered to be light and comfortable, including flex hinges and light, stylish frames. Things like the power slider also look a lot like Meta's glasses, which is a great thing if you want the features but want to avoid Meta.

Samsung Intelligent Eyewear launches this fall and includes 9 hours of battery life on a single charge, a charging case that'll fully recharge the glasses 7 times, a Snapdragon AR1 chipset inside and, of course, deep connectivity with the Galaxy ecosystem.

We've got a full hands-on with Samsung's Intelligent Eyewear for you to check out, including the charging case, several styles, and all the great features you can expect when they launch this fall.

And while it's probably the least exciting launch of the show, the Galaxy Z Flip 8 is still plenty cool, especially with that new Horizon Lock camcorder upgrade. Check out our full Galaxy Z Flip 8 hands-on here!

And that wraps it up for Samsung's July 2026 Galaxy Unpacked show! What did you think? Were any of the new devices particularly exciting? I'm personally most excited about the Fold 8, as I was a huge fan of the original Pixel Fold's shorter, wider design and got quite annoyed when the company switched to the narrower, taller design a year later. Hopefully, this is the beginning of a bunch of new shorter, wider folds!

Looking to preorder any of the new Folds or Flips? We've got the deals you're looking for! Head to our Galaxy Z Fold 8 deals hub to get all the best preorder deals you'll find on the web, or check out the Galaxy Z Flip 8 deals hub if flip-style phones are more your thing.

One of the most surprising things announced today is the inclusion of silicon-carbon material inside the Galaxy Z Fold 8 and Z Fold 8 Ultra's batteries. Samsung has been notoriously conservative with battery upgrades ever since the Galaxy Note 7, and it was anyone's best guess as to when Samsung would finally include silicon-carbon in its phones.

Now, the Z Fold 8 series only has 5% silicon-carbon content, which sounds like a very small amount, but that turns into a big result when you compare the numbers. The Z Fold 7 has a paltry 4,400mAh battery, which was one of the biggest complaints about the phone, but the Galaxy Z Fold 8 Ultra ramps this up to 5,000mAh without increasing the size or weight of the device. That's all thanks to silicon-carbon, and it's wonderful to see Samsung finally start including it!

While there's a definite gulf in the specs between Samsung's latest watches, both of them look surprisingly similar. This shouldn't come as too much of a surprise, considering this was the case with the Galaxy Watch 8 and the Galaxy Watch Ultra, but I still thought it was a little odd that Samsung didn't offer for at least one different option.

In the past, some companies have offered different styles of watches to help people choose one that better fits their style. With these recent releases, Samsung seems to be leaning more into a rugged style, ignoring customers who might be looking for something a little gentler looking. Oh well, I guess that's what the Pixel Watch is for.

Samsung's upcoming smart glasses seem to be completely avoiding a "proper" product name, and it's driving us a little crazy. Are these Samsung Glasses? Galaxy Glasses? Samsung Google Intelligent Eyewear? Warby Parker and/or Gentle Monster AI Glasses? Intelligent Eyewear seems to be the term Google and Samsung use most often, so that's likely what we're going to see at retail.

Regardless of what they're called, I'm elated with the idea of having Gemini built into a pair of AI glasses. I use my Meta-branded smart glasses all the time as a hands-free camera to capture family memories, as it's just so much better than holding onto a phone and my kids seeing me stare at yet another screen.

They're also great for calls and audio, and given Gemini's deep ties into all the Google services I use daily, I might actually start using the AI-based features more than I do on Meta glasses, which often are still fairly limited because Meta doesn't have the same level of access that Google does.

Rumor has it that this year is the last year Samsung will make a new Z Flip phone. I'm not sure I believe that just yet, but given the weirdness in the tech industry right now (with supplies and prices), I suppose anything is possible. After all, Samsung held just 15% of the global foldable market share in Q2 2026, beating Motorola by just 2%.

Considering Motorola only made flip-style phones until the excellent Razr Fold showed up in May this year, it's clear that Samsung isn't selling as many Flips as it should be. With the new wide Fold reportedly taking over the bulk of the sales and waning interest in Samsung's Flip-style phones, I'll be curious to see if this one turns out to be true.

Either way, we've got some great new cover screen features on this year's Z Flip 8, and the excellent Horizon Lock feature is going to make camcorder mode even more fun than it was before.

  • Get a FREE Samsung Galaxy Z Fold 8 with new line, trade-in at T-Mobile

We’ve seen a lot of Galaxy Z Fold 8 preorder deals come down the pipeline since the phones were unveiled this morning, but few offers will actually get you the premium foldable for free. That said, if you’re a T-Mobile customer (or willing to switch), you can get a free Galaxy Z Fold 8 by trading in an old or broken phone and adding a line with the Experience Beyond or Experience More plan.

Sure, it’s a lot of fine print (and the qualified unlimited plans aren't exactly budget-friendly), but if you need a new wireless plan and you agree to these terms, you’re looking at $1,900 of potential savings. That’s nothing to scoff at!

Of course, T-Mobile isn’t the only company offering preorder deals for the new Galaxy phones. If you don’t want to deal with all of the confusing contracts and eligibility requirements, you can also buy the Galaxy Z Fold 8 or Z Fold Ultra unlocked from Amazon and get a free gift card with a balance of up to $350. With phones this expensive, every penny counts!

The Galaxy Z Fold 8 is clearly an eye-catching device, and strange naming decisions aside, it's refreshing that Samsung decided to do something new with this generation rather than churn out nearly-identical devices year after year.

Of course, good-looking devices deserve good-looking protection, so it's worth checking out our guide to the best Galaxy Z Fold 8 cases. The phone's wide design gives you more surface area to work with, after all, so consider it an opportunity to express yourself.

  • Samsung Galaxy Watch 9 40mm (BT): $379.99, plus free $50 gift card at Amazon

The foldable phones may be getting all of the attention, but they're far from the only devices that were announced this morning. The Galaxy Watch 9 is likely to rank among the best Android smartwatches released this year, and Amazon will already give you a free $50 gift card when you preorder one of these wearables from its website.

Just like the other devices that Samsung announced today, the Galaxy Watch 9 will officially hit store shelves on August 7th.

  • Samsung Galaxy Z Fold 8 256GB: $1,900 FREE with eligible trade-in, qualified line at AT&T

Not one to be outdone by the competition, AT&T has also just launched a deal that could get you a free Samsung Galaxy Z Fold 8 when you add a line with an eligible unlimited plan and trade in an old or broken phone. That's $1,900 of potential savings!

To be honest, I wish all of these preorder deals didn't involve so much fine print, but with prices on tech going up all across the board, I'll take just about any offer I can get.

If you're sick of all the trade-in opportunities and free gift card deals, you might be wondering when the Galaxy Z Fold 8 series (and everything else announced today) will finally get a good old-fashioned discount.

Well, it might be a while. As the eCommerce Editor of Android Central, I've covered a lot of device launches over the years, and it usually takes a few months before a new Samsung phone gets any real price drop. There's a chance that we'll see some decent discounts when the fall sales arrive in September/October; otherwise, we'll have to wait for Black Friday.

In the meantime, keep your eyes peeled: it's possible that we'll see some good deals on lingering Galaxy Z Fold 7 / Z Flip 7 stock in the coming days.

If anything, the Galaxy Z Fold 7 may be a better choice if it gets a decent discount. Prices of all tech products have increased substantially in recent months, and with the Z Fold 8 Ultra starting at $2,099, it's worth taking a look at last year's devices. Same goes for the Z Flip 7 as well.

As a reminder, the new foldables and smartwatches are going on sale starting August 7. Samsung has unmatched global presence, and the new products are available in every major country. That said, you don't get trade-in deals that are quite as generous as the U.S. in most regions.

Out of all the devices that Samsung unveiled yesterday, the Galaxy Z Fold 8 is the one I want to use the most. The wider profile looks interesting, and the fact that it's designed to consume media and read books makes it a great choice for my own use case. At this point, there's no telling if the foldable will gain anywhere close to the same level of mainstream attention as the book-style devices Samsung has been selling, but I want it to do well.

We haven't talked about the software much; all the foldables run One UI 9 based on Android 17 out of the box. Like their predecessors, they will get seven Android OS updates. Samsung isn't changing its interface or adding much in the way of features with One UI 9, but there is a generous smattering of Galaxy AI everywhere. It's clear that Samsung is positioning AI as a key point of differentiation on its devices, and to its credit, it has a strong suite of utilities.

As is the case with the last few iterations of Android, Samsung's One UI 9 looks quite different to Android 17 on a Pixel. This is by design; Samsung has always maintained its own visual identity, and One UI 9 continues that trend. That said, you get most of the latest features Google introduced in Android 17 — other than the Bubbles floating windows mode. That's limited to the Pixel Launcher, but other than the multitasking feature, all the security and privacy features along with all the new changes in Android 17 are also available in One UI 9.

Samsung delivered a strong set of upgrades this year, and it had to do so. With Motorola launching the Razr Fold and Google set to introduce its 2026 foldable next month, Samsung needed to maintain the momentum in this category, and the Galaxy Z Fold 8 and Fold 8 Ultra are the right products to do so.

It's exciting that Samsung finally used silicon battery tech in its latest foldables. Outside of the Razr Fold, there isn't another foldable in the U.S. that leverages this tech, and it's great to see Samsung making the switch. Of course, the brand didn't put an oversized battery — unlike most Chinese brands — but it still means we get bigger batteries than last year in thinner designs, and that's an overall positive.

item.title: Garmin's turn: the CIRQA is a screenless band, and you get all-day help without a subscription
item.description: Garmin launched its take on the screenless fitness band: the CIRQA, and users should know that everything it provides is available without a subscription.
item.author: nickodiaz@sbcglobal.net (Nickolas Diaz)
item.dc:creator: Nickolas Diaz
item.dc:description: null

media:content.media:description: The Garmin CIRQA fitness band in multiple colors, like black, blue, and mauve.
media:content.media:text: The Garmin CIRQA fitness band in multiple colors, like black, blue, and mauve.
media:content.media:title: The Garmin CIRQA fitness band in multiple colors, like black, blue, and mauve.

item.content:encoded:
What you need to know

  • Garmin announced the CIRQA, its take on the screenless fitness band, which delivers a wealth of health and fitness insights without a subscription.
  • The CIRQA can track more than 80 activities, while also focusing on women's health, sleep score, HRV, skin temperature, and much more.
  • Garmin's store listing says the CIRQA is available for $199; however, consumers can purchase it from its storefront on July 24.

Garmin is getting in on the screenless fitness band trend this week. The company highlights its strong wellness capabilities, and they're all possible without a subscription.

Early this morning (July 21), Garmin announced in a press release that its first screenless fitness band, the CIRQA, is available now for $199. As we've already said, the CIRQA is screenless, and Garmin says it's the device's "inconspicuous design" that helps "minimize distractions while tracking important health and performance metrics." The band will automatically detect and record a multitude of activities.

While the expected metrics will be recorded (steps, calories burned, etc.), Garmin says the CIRQA can track more than 80 activities, such as running, walking, and yoga. What's more, users can "select a favorite activity to track by simply tapping the single side button." Garmin is giving the CIRQA band additional strength to handle "advanced training." The band will help inform users if they should push themselves or take a rest day.

It will provide HRV status, VO2 Max, and more for insights about your training effectiveness. Understanding your recovery time is another aspect of the band's fitness assistance. For users going on runs or long walks, the CIRQA supports GPS when connected to a compatible Android or iPhone.

All about your health

Health monitoring—all-day, especially—is where Garmin takes us next. Important metrics, such as "wrist-based heart rate, Body Battery energy monitoring, Pulse Ox2, stress, and skin temperature," are available with the CIRQA. For women's health, Garmin says users will find menstrual cycle and pregnancy tracking. Each provides enhanced insight into period predictions and past ovulation estimates by using skin temperature readings while sleeping.

For all users, sleep data the fitness band has captured provides a detailed look into their sleep stages, sleep score, heart rate variability, respiration, and nap detection. Every captured statistic is available to users to view in the Garmin Connect app.

We mentioned this before, but the health and fitness features the CIRQA can provide are available to consumers without a subscription. Garmin's vice president of consumer sales and marketing, Susan Lyman, said, "Plus, with no subscription required, CIRQA Smart Band helps you stay on top of your health and fitness goals—and makes a thoughtful gift for others who want to do the same."

The CIRQA fitness band has been designed with all-day comfort in mind. Garmin highlights its fabric material and "fun, neutral colors." The band is available in Citron Gray, Mauve, French Gray, Dark Olive, Captain Blue, French Blue, and black. Consumers can wear the CIRQA on their wrist or as an armband.

Once again, Garmin states the CIRQA fitness band is available for $199. Its store listing says consumers can begin purchasing it on July 24.

item.title: Xbox Game Pass joins Meta Horizon Plus, and to be honest, I'm thrilled
item.description: Meta Horizon Plus welcomes the Xbox Game Pass for its Quest VR users, opening the doors to a huge library of additional games.
item.author: nickodiaz@sbcglobal.net (Nickolas Diaz)
item.dc:creator: Nickolas Diaz
item.dc:description: null

media:content.media:description: The Meta Horizon app on an Honor Magic V5 smartphone in front of a Meta Quest 3 VR headset
media:content.media:text: The Meta Horizon app on an Honor Magic V5 smartphone in front of a Meta Quest 3 VR headset
media:content.media:title: The Meta Horizon app on an Honor Magic V5 smartphone in front of a Meta Quest 3 VR headset

item.content:encoded:
What you need to know

  • Meta has collaborated with Xbox once again, stating it's bringing the "starter edition" Xbox Game Pass into its Horizon Plus subscription for VR gaming.
  • The Xbox Game Pass opens up an additional 50 plus library of games, ranging from AAA to indie titles on its Quest headsets.
  • The starter edition Xbox Game Pass for Meta Horizon Plus is rolling out today (July 21).

Meta Horizon Plus is a gateway into VR games on the company's Quest headsets. We're always looking to expand our libraries, and Meta's teaming up with Xbox to do so.

This morning (July 21), it was announced that Meta Horizon Plus and Xbox Game Pass are teaming up. Specifically, Meta says the "starter edition" of the Xbox Game Pass will award VR users with "a library of 50+ games." The two companies have included a mix of titles, ranging from popular AAA games to indie titles. The press release says users will find Fallout 4 and Fallout 76 from Bethesda Softworks, Overcooked 2 (chaotic, co-op cooking game) from Ghost Town Games, Deep Rock Galactic, and more.

What's more, Meta says the starter edition of the Xbox Game Pass will deliver 10 hours of cloud gaming "each month." This lets users stream their games, not download. Users can play with others using cloud and PC multiplayer. Don't worry; Meta says there will be an option to "download titles from the library for unlimited play."

Synced progression is another major aspect.

If you've been gaming on your Meta VR headset and have swapped to your Xbox, your game progress (saved data) will remain. This will remain constant if you're gaming on an Xbox, PC, Meta Quest headset, or another supported device. Users have chances to earn additional rewards by playing, too. Xbox Game Pass for the Meta Horizon Plus subscription is rolling out today (July 21).

Meta adds, "Your Meta Horizon+ subscription also comes with exclusive deals to help you fill out your Meta Quest library. It’s all yours for just $59.99 USD per year, or $7.99 USD per month. You can even try Meta Horizon+ for a month on us."

Meta & Xbox: once again

There's more to experience soon. Meta says it's working on a way to let users emulate an Xbox gamepad with its Quest Touch controllers. Users can expect this support to roll out over the next few weeks. However, for the time being, users can play their Xbox games on their Quest headset without a controller, or users can pair their controller with their headset for a "traditional experience."

Meta and Xbox together for the sake of games isn't anything new. If you happened to miss it, the two companies collaborated on a Meta Quest 3S Xbox Edition in June 2025. This headset was truly a limited edition device. Meta's storefront sold out of them quickly, and many consumers were faced with Best Buy or scalpers (yikes). The Xbox Edition Quest 3S wasn't much different from the standard model. The packaging is where they're different.

There's the classic green and black color scheme from Xbox, as well as a "special edition preinstalled Elite Strap." Consumers received an Xbox controller included with the purchase, which was already paired with the system. Turning it on and playing is always the way to go, and that's what this offered.

Android Central's Take

The emulation for the Meta Touch controllers is pretty cool. Although, I do enjoy classic controller-style gameplay. I typically play on a PC nowadays, but I've been playing on my console more, too. Playing with a controller, lounging. There's nothing better. Meta bringing the Xbox Game Pass into their Horizon Plus subscription service is also a major win for people who love VR and gaming on a VR device. Xbox Game Pass has a pretty good selection of games. If I had one, I'd definitely do it.

item.title: Need a new laptop for Back-to-School season? Best Buy is offering up to 60% OFF Lenovo, HP, and Acer Chromebooks during its Black Friday in July sale
item.description: Best Buy's Black Friday in July sale is here, showcasing some of the year's best deals on class-ready Chromebooks.
item.author: patrick.farmer@futurenet.com (Patrick Farmer)
item.dc:creator: Patrick Farmer
item.dc:description: <p>Patrick (he/him) is the eCommerce Editor of Android Central. After working in independent bookstores for the better part of a decade and using freelance writing as a side hustle, Patrick switched to writing full time in 2020. Patrick’s writing has appeared in the Chicago Tribune, The Hill, BestReviews, OddityMall, and many other publications across the web. He joined Android Central as the Deals and Commerce Writer in 2022 and now runs the site’s eCommerce efforts and coverage of major sale events. Although he’s written about everything from exercise equipment to turntables, Patrick’s work nowadays is primarily concerned with saving people money on tech. He’s particularly passionate about eliminating buyer’s remorse and taking subjects that can be confusing for shoppers — such as switching wireless carriers or buying a new smart TV — and breaking them down into simple, easy-to-understand terms. Whether you’re a veteran tech-head or a novice, Patrick offers honest, no-nonsense shopping advice with years of hands-on experience to back it up. When he isn’t hunting down the best discounts or digging through the fine print of a carrier deal, Patrick enjoys trekking around the Rocky Mountains, camping out at a brewery, or spending time with his beloved polydactyl cat. &nbsp;</p>

media:content.media:description: Lenovo IdeaPad Slim 3 Chromebook review
media:content.media:text: Lenovo IdeaPad Slim 3 Chromebook review
media:content.media:title: Lenovo IdeaPad Slim 3 Chromebook review

item.content:encoded:
Back to school season is upon us, and if you're in the market for a new laptop, you'll be pleased to learn that Best Buy is currently launching some of the year's best Chromebook deals as part of its Black Friday in July sale. This sitewide sale event kicked off on July 20th and is set to run through Sunday, July 26th, which means you have less than a week to explore some of the season's best tech discounts before they disappear.

Never owned a Chromebook before? These simple laptops are ideal for students because they're affordable, sturdy, and packed with decent enough specs that they can handle the majority of basic tasks with ease. They're not all built equally, however. Some Chromebooks may be designed for durability so you can easily throw them into a backpack and carry them with you wherever you go, while others are constructed more like tablets, with convertible designs, touchscreen displays, and included styli.

Needless to say, we've tested dozens of Chromebooks over the years, so I can verify that all of the discounted laptops listed below are worthy of your wallet. Whether you want a device for studying, streaming, or simply surfing the web, keep reading for all of my favorite Chromebook deals from Best Buy's Black Friday in July sale.

item.title: The 'stunning, behemoth' Galaxy Tab S10 Ultra just scored a $350 discount during Best Buy's Black Friday in July sale
item.description: The Samsung Galaxy Tab S10 Ultra still holds up in 2026—especially at this price.
item.dc:creator: Zachary David
item.dc:description: null

media:content.media:description: Samsung Galaxy Tab S10 Ultra S Pen and Cameras
media:content.media:text: Samsung Galaxy Tab S10 Ultra S Pen and Cameras
media:content.media:title: Samsung Galaxy Tab S10 Ultra S Pen and Cameras

item.content:encoded:
Sometimes the best way to swing a tablet deal is to go with a last-gen option, especially if you're looking at devices in the premium price range. As one example, Best Buy has knocked $350 off the price of the Samsung Galaxy Tab S10 Ultra, bringing it down to just $850.

While the newer Galaxy Tab S11 Ultra is the best premium Android tablet there is today, the S10 Ultra sports relatively similar specs, with a large, good-looking OLED display and a huge battery. It's not exactly the sleekest device, weighing in at 692 pounds, but it's super powerful, it includes the first-party Samsung S Pen, and a microSD card slot for added storage.

This deal is available for the base-level, 256GB configuration of the S10 Ultra, though you can also forego the deal and upgrade to either 512GB or 1TB. Additionally, the deal is available for the S10 Ultra's Moonstone Gray option, although the Platinum Silver version is currently sold out.

  • See the full list of deals at Best Buy's Black Friday in July sale

Samsung Galaxy Tab S10 Ultra: $1,199.99 $849.99 at Best Buy

As part of its Black Friday in July sale, Best Buy is offering the Samsung Galaxy Tab S10 Ultra for just $850, marking $350 in savings. The tablet comes with the well-liked S Pen, 256GB of storage, and a massive screen that buyers love.View Deal

✅Recommended if: you prefer a large tablet with a stylus included for digital drawing, note-taking, and more; you have a budget of around $850 for a premium-level tablet; you want a tablet with a lot of processing power and a large, good-looking display.

❌Skip this deal if: you'd rather upgrade to the S11 Ultra and you have the budget to do so; you like having a small tablet that's easy to hold and use while couch surfing; you'd prefer to go even cheaper than $850 and you don't need all the bells and whistles of a premium device.

The Samsung Galaxy Tab S10 Ultra has a powerful MediaTek Dimensity 9300 Plus processor, 12GB of RAM, and a beautiful, 14.6-inch AMOLED display with a 120Hz refresh rate. It's definitely a hefty device, but most don't seem to mind the weight of the massive 11,200mAh battery, especially when considering the benefits it offers to battery life and 45W fast-charging.

As for cameras, the device includes 12MP wide and ultrawide front lenses, and 13MP wide and 8MP ultrawide rear-facing lenses. The first-party Samsung S Pen is also well-liked by users, making note-taking and digital drawing easy.

item.title: Halliday’s smart glasses want to fix your meetings before they go off the rails
item.description: Halliday G2 puts AI in your meetings with smart glasses that deliver live insights, though its always-listening mic may leave privacy-conscious users thinking twice.
item.author: techkritiko@gmail.com (Jay Bonggolto)
item.dc:creator: Jay Bonggolto
item.dc:description: <p>Jay Bonggolto always keeps a nose for news. He has been writing about consumer tech and apps for as long as he can remember, and he has used a variety of Android phones since falling in love with Jelly Bean. When he's not writing, he likes to spend time outside, stealing scenes with his phone camera. Send him a direct message via X or LinkedIn.</p>

media:content.media:description: Halliday G2 smart glasses in black beside its case on a table
media:content.media:text: Halliday G2 smart glasses in black beside its case on a table
media:content.media:title: Halliday G2 smart glasses in black beside its case on a table

item.content:encoded:
What you need to know

  • Halliday G2 uses the company's new Meeting Flow feature to provide real-time prompts and context as conversations happen.
  • Meeting Flow helps you stay engaged without reaching for your phone, offering live captions, translations, instant recaps, contextual research, and confidence-rated answers through the glasses.
  • The AI spots topic drift, flags unclear decisions, identifies incomplete action items, and generates structured summaries with transcripts after meetings.

Most AI meeting assistants have become pretty good at telling you what already happened. Halliday thinks that is solving the wrong problem and has unveiled the Halliday G2, a new breed of AI smart glasses based on a feature called Meeting Flow, which gives you live prompts and contextual information during a conversation rather than producing notes after everyone’s left the room.

The new glasses are available for pre-order starting today with a $10 deposit, shaving $100 off the expected retail price of $599 before shipments are scheduled for September 2026.

Halliday’s pitch is that the glasses’ Meeting Flow listens to the conversation in real time and presents bite-sized insights directly through the glasses’ waveguide display rather than having users type prompts into an AI chatbot. That includes live captions and translation, instant recaps if you lose track of the discussion, and contextual research that can answer questions without having to explain the whole situation to an AI first. The answers also include confidence ratings, and the full source material is still available in the companion app.

Meeting Flow can spot off-topic conversations, alert you to ambiguous decisions before they become the default, identify commitments missing an owner or due date, and maintain a running checklist of open items so they don’t disappear once everyone says goodbye. When the meeting is over, the companion app will generate a structured summary of the meeting, including confirmed decisions, assigned tasks, open items, and transcripts.

The hardware is just as workplace-oriented. The previous generation’s display has been replaced by binocular waveguide optics powered by dual micro-LED projectors capable of up to 1,600 nits of brightness and 600 x 300 resolution per eye in the Halliday G2. The four-microphone array can pick up voice from a distance of up to two meters, while Halliday is aiming for more than 12 hours of typical battery life and a weight of less than 50 grams.

Android Central's Take

There are clear benefits to having the ability to instantly pull up action items and keep meetings on track without having to check a phone, but I remain fiercely skeptical. I’m all for a camera-free design, but strapping an always-on microphone to my face that feeds every boardroom whisper into an AI feels like a data privacy nightmare.

Importantly, there is no camera, which could make the glasses more acceptable in meetings, secure workplaces, and other privacy-sensitive environments, while also helping reduce weight and extend battery life.

Outside the office, the glasses also let you get notifications, take phone calls, control music, see dashboard cards, translate in real time in over 45 languages, deploy a built-in AI assistant, and access a teleprompter-style Cheatsheet mode for notes and reminders.

Halliday says future updates to Meeting Flow through 2026 will add deeper contextual awareness, enabling AI to better understand ongoing projects, people, and previous conversations.

item.title: Gboard could have a huge accessibility feature on the way for sign language
item.description: Google is allegedly working on a significant accessibility feature for Gboard and users who communicate via sign language.
item.author: nickodiaz@sbcglobal.net (Nickolas Diaz)
item.dc:creator: Nickolas Diaz
item.dc:description: null

media:content.media:description: Gboard for Android on a Google Pixel 6
media:content.media:text: Gboard for Android on a Google Pixel 6
media:content.media:title: Gboard for Android on a Google Pixel 6

item.content:encoded:
What you need to know

  • A tipster reportedly discovered a new accessibility feature that Google is working on for Gboard called "Sign-to-text."
  • This feature would leverage the user's camera and Google's AI to "translate" sign language into written text for messages or online searches and more.
  • Android has several common accessibility features, and Android 16 helped to refine the hearing aid experience for Bluetooth LE-based models and more.

There are a few important accessibility features present on Android that help make devices useful. However, a recent rumor claims that Google is working on another, and it could be its most significant.

"Sign-to-text" is a feature that Android Authority claims Google is working on for its keyboard app, Gboard. Within the code of a recent Gboard app version, the publication claims it found a welcome pop-up for "Sign-to-text," an accessibility feature for users who communicate via sign language. The early description says it will "translate your signing into text messages, web searches, emails, and more."

As one might imagine, this Gboard feature would leverage your phone's camera to capture you signing. If you also expect this feature to rely on Google's AI, you'd be right. Together with tipster AssembleDebug, the publication found that Sign-to-text will "translate" sign language into written text. While the video is said to "never leave your device," Sign-to-text will send "points representing the movement of your body" to Google for translation before deletion.

There's still quite a bit unknown about this. The tipster couldn't find much else aside from the splash screen and brief description. There's also speculation about how good Google's support for sign language will be. Android Authority highlights that there are other sign languages out there aside from American Sign Language.

Accessible for all

As we mentioned before, Android has its fair share of accessibility options for users. Android 16 made two important changes for users who rely on hearing aids. If a user has a pair of Bluetooth LE earbuds, they can switch to their phone's mic to hear a call better. Additionally, users can adjust the volume of their hearing aids from their phone. Work to improve the hearing aid experience continued for Android 16 in December 2025.

In Google's year-end update, hearing aids were given Fast Pair support. The single-tap experience for pairing these devices to a compatible Android device was undoubtedly a major win. Google rolled this out for Demant hearing aids first, but planned to expand its support to Starkey devices earlier this year. Other than hearing aids, Android has accessibility options for colors and text size, but sign language is something fresh for its available selection.

Android Central's Take

I like that this has been discovered. It's in Google's code, yeah, but hopefully that means something. I've seen things like this disappear, as it's probably something Google is trying to see if it can do. I truly hope this is one that makes it because it would be huge for people who sign.

item.title: How to make your Android safer without changing how you use it
item.description: A practical guide to reducing Android security risks with minimal effort
item.author: tips@androidcentral.com (Sponsored)
item.dc:creator: Sponsored
item.dc:description: null

media:content.media:description: Group of young people looking at a phone, laughing
media:content.media:text: Group of young people looking at a phone, laughing
media:content.media:title: Group of young people looking at a phone, laughing

item.content:encoded:
Smartphones have become the place where people manage their money, store their memories, organise their travel plans, access work accounts and stay connected with friends and family.

With so much personal information concentrated in one place, Android security has naturally become more important than ever, but luckily, strengthening protection doesn't have to involve dramatic lifestyle changes, technical expertise, or constantly worrying about the latest online threat.

In fact, some of the most effective ways to improve security occur in the background, helping users stay protected while they continue to browse, message, shop, and work exactly as they always have.

TL;DR: Android security is most effective when it works without disrupting everyday usage. Features such as web protection across apps, scam filtering, secure browsing on public Wi-Fi and background threat monitoring help reduce risk while requiring minimal user intervention. Bitdefender Mobile Security for Android focuses on protecting Android devices from modern threats, while Bitdefender Total Security extends protection across multiple devices and platforms.

Keep Android software up to date

Keeping Android and installed apps updated remains one of the easiest ways to improve security.

Software updates often include fixes for newly discovered vulnerabilities, performance improvements, and security enhancements, which means delaying updates can leave devices exposed to risks already addressed by developers. Most Android devices can install updates automatically, making this a simple set-and-forget step that requires very little effort on your part.

Updates help close security gaps before they can be exploited and ensure compatibility with the latest security protections built into Android itself. For users who rely on their phones for work, banking or online shopping, staying current with updates is one of the simplest security habits available.

Scam links increasingly appear inside everyday apps

Many people still associate online threats with suspicious emails, but modern scams often arrive through messaging platforms and social apps.

A fake parcel delivery notification, a fraudulent account warning, or a convincing promotional offer can appear in a text message, WhatsApp chat, or a social media direct message. These scams are often designed to create a sense of urgency, encouraging you to act before you have time to think.

Treat unexpected links with caution, even when they appear to come from familiar companies. Protection tools that scan links across apps can add an extra layer of security, which is particularly useful because many people now spend more time in messaging and social apps than in traditional web browsers.

Bitdefender Mobile Security for Android includes web protection that works across apps, helping identify potentially harmful links wherever they appear, making it especially useful for people who regularly communicate through messaging platforms throughout the day.

Public Wi-Fi still deserves caution

Free Wi-Fi is convenient, especially while travelling or working remotely, where airports, hotels, cafés and train stations all offer quick internet access when mobile data isn't available.

However, public networks can present some of the gravest privacy and security concerns these days. You rarely know who manages a network, how it’s configured or who else may be connected to it, an uncertainty that creates opportunities for cybercriminals to intercept data or create fake networks designed to capture personal information.

Public Wi-Fi can be used safely, but additional protection is recommended when accessing sensitive information.

Secure browsing tools can help protect personal data when using unfamiliar networks, which is particularly valuable for frequent travellers, remote workers and anyone who regularly connects to Wi-Fi outside the home.

The reality is that most people will continue to use public Wi-Fi, but you can reduce your exposure while maintaining convenience by being mindful of your security options.

Review app permissions regularly

Many Android apps request access to location data, photos, contacts, microphones or camera, and while some requests are entirely legitimate, others might be questionable.

A navigation app needing location access to function correctly is an understandable request. However, a simple utility app requesting broad access to personal information may warrant further investigation.

Location, microphone, camera, and contact permissions typically require close attention because they involve highly personal information.

Android provides increasingly detailed permission controls, allowing you to decide what apps can access and when. A quick review of app permissions every few months can help identify software that may have accumulated access it no longer needs.

Security works best when it stays in the background

One reason people ignore security recommendations is that they assume protection requires constant management, whereas in reality, modern mobile security solutions are designed to work quietly behind the scenes.

Threat monitoring, malicious app detection and scam filtering can all operate in the background while you focus on your daily activities, whether it’s answering messages while commuting, checking emails between meetings or browsing online while watching television.

Security tools that demand constant attention often become frustrating – protection that works automatically is far more likely to remain active over the long term.

Bitdefender Mobile Security for Android is designed around this principle, helping protect devices while requiring minimal day-to-day management, making it particularly useful for people who want stronger security without added complexity.

Consider protection beyond your phone

For many households, smartphones are only one part of a much larger digital ecosystem that includes laptops, desktop PCs, and tablets, often containing the same accounts, files and personal information found on mobile devices.

A security strategy that focuses exclusively on a single device can leave other parts of the household exposed, which is why multi-device protection is particularly useful for families, remote workers, and people who regularly switch between devices throughout the day.

You might start the morning by checking emails on a laptop, browsing social media on a phone during lunch and streaming content from a tablet in the evening. Each of your devices represents a potential entry point for security threats.

This is where the Bitdefender Total Security comes into play. It’s designed to provide broader protection across multiple devices, helping you manage security more consistently across all your hardware without adding complexity.

Small security improvements add up

Making an Android device safer requires no dramatic changes and doesn’t mean you have to stop using public Wi-Fi, avoid messaging apps, or become a cybersecurity specialist.

Simple habits such as keeping software updated, reviewing app permissions, staying cautious around unexpected links and using security tools that operate quietly in the background can significantly reduce risk over time.

Modern Android security is increasingly about supporting everyday behaviour, and the best protection often works silently, helping you navigate daily digital life with greater confidence and fewer interruptions.

  • Explore how Bitdefender security solutions can help protect your devices, identity and privacy — all in one place.

item.title: Google's latest Android 17 beta fixes Bluetooth headaches and Gemini crashes
item.description: Android 17 QPR2 Beta 1 is here, fixing Bluetooth, Gemini, and touch bugs on Pixels, though Pixel 6 users are officially reaching the end of the beta road.
item.author: techkritiko@gmail.com (Jay Bonggolto)
item.dc:creator: Jay Bonggolto
item.dc:description: <p>Jay Bonggolto always keeps a nose for news. He has been writing about consumer tech and apps for as long as he can remember, and he has used a variety of Android phones since falling in love with Jelly Bean. When he's not writing, he likes to spend time outside, stealing scenes with his phone camera. Send him a direct message via X or LinkedIn.</p>

media:content.media:description: Android 17 Easter egg on Pixel 10, 10 Pro XL and Pixel 10 Pro Fold
media:content.media:text: Android 17 Easter egg on Pixel 10, 10 Pro XL and Pixel 10 Pro Fold
media:content.media:title: Android 17 Easter egg on Pixel 10, 10 Pro XL and Pixel 10 Pro Fold

item.content:encoded:
What you need to know

  • Android 17 QPR2 Beta 1 is officially here, kicking off testing for Google's next Pixel Feature Drop arriving in December 2026.
  • This beta includes fixes for Bluetooth reconnection failures, lock screen media glitches, Gemini-related reboot crashes, and touch input bugs.
  • The update supports Pixel 6a through the Pixel 10 lineup, plus the Pixel Fold and Pixel Tablet, and is available via the Android Beta Program and Android Emulator.

Google’s not wasting any time after wrapping up Android 17 QPR1. The company has officially kicked off testing of Android 17 QPR2 Beta 1, offering Pixel users and developers a first look at the next quarterly update expected to arrive as the December 2026 Feature Drop.

This release doesn’t come with any new user-facing features, but it does fix some annoying bugs that have plagued Pixel owners. In its official release notes, Google says it has fixed an issue where Bluetooth devices could fail to reconnect after losing pairing, with no indication there was a problem. There’s also a fix that stops media controls from flashing briefly on the lock screen when waking a phone, even if notifications for that app had been turned off.

The most important fix is maybe for Gemini. Pixel devices may suddenly reboot when launching Google’s AI assistant due to a system crash. Google also addressed an issue with touch input where apps would stop receiving touch events after users performed a drag-and-drop gesture with multiple fingers.

The Android 17 QPR2 Beta 1 is now available with build number CP41.260701.005 and supports most of the supported Pixels, including the Pixel 6a, Pixel 7 series, Pixel Fold, Pixel Tablet, Pixel 8 series, Pixel 9 family, and the latest Pixel 10 devices. It’s also available through the Android Emulator, and users enrolled in the program can install it over the air via the Android Beta Program.

But the rollout brings a major change, too. If you still have a Pixel 6 or 6 Pro, your beta journey ends right here. These devices have reached their guaranteed update limits, so the previous QPR1 branch is their final beta track.

Android Central's Take

I don’t mind if Google takes a break from dumping flashy new features on us if it means they’ll actually iron out the bugs that get in the way of using my phone. Not getting access to yet another AI gimmick is 100x more annoying than Gemini demanding a reboot or Bluetooth suddenly refusing to reconnect. That said, I’d still like to see Google spend less time playing catch-up with stability issues in the first place. Pixel owners signed up for a premium Android experience, not a free QA gig. If QPR2 delivers a smoother, more reliable phone by December, that’s a win. But it would be even better if these fixes didn’t have to wait for a quarterly update.

item.title: Leaked Snapdragon 4 Gen 6 specs suggest an upgrade in the graphics department
item.description: A rumor spins an interesting theory for Qualcomm's next budget Snapdragon 3 Gen 6 and its graphical performance.
item.author: nickodiaz@sbcglobal.net (Nickolas Diaz)
item.dc:creator: Nickolas Diaz
item.dc:description: null

media:content.media:description: The Snapdragon 4 Gen 5 SoC helps empower more affordable phones with performance capabilities that make the difference in everyday life.
media:content.media:text: The Snapdragon 4 Gen 5 SoC helps empower more affordable phones with performance capabilities that make the difference in everyday life.
media:content.media:title: The Snapdragon 4 Gen 5 SoC helps empower more affordable phones with performance capabilities that make the difference in everyday life.

item.content:encoded:
What you need to know

  • Rumors about Qualcomm's next budget chip, the Snapdragon 4 gen 6, claim another graphical upgrade is in the works.
  • A report claims the chip could feature an Adreno 6-series GPU, as well as Bluetooth 6.0 and Wi-Fi 6 connectivity.
  • The Snapdragon 4 Gen 5, which launched in May, was the first 4-series chip that could muster 90fps in games with an Adreno 4-series GPU.
Android Central's Take

I'm interested in this graphics rumor for the SD 4 Gen 6 the most. I've had budget phones before, and they've done okay—for a time. Games continue to update. Their graphics continue to evolve. Eventually, that budget phone feels... outmatched. Qualcomm's trying to help by offering a better GPU, and 90fps is a good thing to have. I just can't help but wonder how much further it's willing to push its budget chip's GPU since it's, well, budget.

Qualcomm is supposedly working on another generation of chips for budget phones, and its specs might've appeared online.

Budget phones are typically dropped down a notch when it comes to overall performance. However, alleged details about Qualcomm's next Snapdragon 4 Gen 6 from NotebookCheck suggest the company is trying to close the gap. The post claims the SD 4 Gen 6 could include a much-needed Adreno GPU upgrade. The chip might jump to the Adreno 6 series.

It's suspected that this would deliver yet another significant increase in graphical output for budget phones. As it stands, the publication didn't have concrete numbers. Other supposed upgrades involve its security, which is "getting a refresh." As far as connectivity is concerned, rumors state the SD 4 Gen 6 could feature Bluetooth 6.0 and an upgraded Wi-Fi 6 over Wi-Fi 5 in the Gen 5 SoC.

For what might remain the same, the publication adds that the Snapdragon 4 Gen 6 might keep the same CPU architecture as its predecessor. If that's the case, then we might be in for another 2+6 Kyro core arrangement (Kryo Gold, Kryo Silver). Another similarity could be its process, which NotebookCheck says might remain at 4nm.

Qualcomm released a pair of chips in May, both of which focused on the affordable phone market. Graphics were a focus for Qualcomm with the SD 4 Gen 5. The company states this chip features a "77% increase in Adreno GPU performance for 90fps in games." This was a big moment for Qualcomm. Previously, no 4-series chip achieved such a high FPS (frames per second). Mind you, this is with the Adreno 4-series GPU. With rumors suggesting an upgrade in this department, hopefully Qualcomm can push its graphics even more.

Budget phones often struggle with mobile games with more intense graphics, but knocking them down to medium or low can help. However, if Qualcomm is truly looking to boost its GPU again, maybe users can feel more comfortable with medium graphics across the board without noticeable FPS drops.

The rumors about the Snapdragon 4 Gen 6 didn't have a release date in mind. Considering we got Qualcomm's last wave in May, there's a chance the company could stick to that next year.

item.title: AT&T could raise home internet prices for the people who can least afford it
item.description: AT&T is reportedly hiking legacy Fiber and Internet Air bills by $5 again, leaving customers paying more.
item.author: techkritiko@gmail.com (Jay Bonggolto)
item.dc:creator: Jay Bonggolto
item.dc:description: <p>Jay Bonggolto always keeps a nose for news. He has been writing about consumer tech and apps for as long as he can remember, and he has used a variety of Android phones since falling in love with Jelly Bean. When he's not writing, he likes to spend time outside, stealing scenes with his phone camera. Send him a direct message via X or LinkedIn.</p>

media:content.media:description: AT&T logo
media:content.media:text: AT&T logo
media:content.media:title: AT&T logo

item.content:encoded:
What you need to know

  • AT&T is reportedly raising home internet prices again, with legacy Fiber and Internet Air customers facing a $5 monthly increase starting August 16.
  • Promotional discounts will stay, but affected customers will still pay more because the base monthly rate is increasing.
  • AT&T says the extra revenue funds network and service improvements, though customers have heard that explanation before.

AT&T just can’t seem to stop raising prices, and now its home internet customers may be next. The carrier already raised prices on a handful of wireless plans earlier this year and is reportedly planning a second monthly price hike for some internet customers beginning August 16.

PhoneArena has learned from internal documents that the bill of customers on legacy AT&T Fiber and Internet Air plans will be increased by $5 per month starting in mid-August.

Notices are expected to be included on future billing statements before the changes are implemented. Existing promotional discounts should remain in place, but the higher base price means many subscribers will still end up paying more overall.

It’s a move that’s becoming more and more familiar for AT&T customers. Over the last few months, the carrier has bumped up prices on several legacy wireless plans, tacked on more to the rates of retired Unlimited Your Way offerings, and even raised its Administrative and Regulatory Cost Recovery Fee by $1 per line.

AT&T’s explanation, too, sounds familiar. Higher prices help support continued investments in the company’s network, service quality, and customer experience, internal documents say. It's the same explanation it's used for past increases in wireless prices. The question is whether customers will see enough added value to warrant another bill increase.

Android Central's Take

If I’m paying more every few months, I’d like to see something tangible in return for that, rather than the usual promise of “network investments.” Faster speeds, wider coverage, or some meaningful new perks would make the extra $5 easier to swallow. Instead, customers are left wondering again whether they’re being rewarded for loyalty or just taxed.

AT&T has been touting newer offerings, such as its customizable Build-A-Plan service that now bundles wireless and home internet under a single subscription. The carrier isn’t forcing a move, but increasing the price of older plans could naturally push some subscribers to look at newer options.

If you have AT&T internet, look for a price notice on your next bill. The hike won’t be equal for everyone, but if you have an older Fiber or Internet Air plan, your monthly bill could soon jump by another $5. At this point, you may want to compare your current plan to newer offerings, or even competing providers, to see if sticking around still makes financial sense.
